objects.name: CyberNetSec.io
objects.description: CyberNetSec.io is a cybersecurity intelligence platform providing timely, accurate, deduplicated, and actionable threat intelligence for security professionals. The platform aggregates, deduplicates, and enriches threat data from diverse public sources, mapping all threats to MITRE ATT&CK and D3FEND frameworks with operationally-focused analysis, detection guidance, and mitigation recommendations. Operated by security professionals, for the security community.

objects.name: Jason Gomes
objects.description: Cybersecurity practitioner and founder of CyberNetSec.io with over 10 years of specialized experience in security operations, threat intelligence, incident response, and security automation. Expertise spans SOAR/XSOAR orchestration, threat intelligence platforms, SIEM/UEBA analytics, and cyber fusion centers.

objects.name: OceanLotus
objects.description: A sophisticated threat actor aligned with Vietnamese state interests that has pivoted its operations to focus on domestic targets, including a construction firm and stock market investors. The campaigns are noted to potentially align with Vietnam's national anti-corruption efforts.
goals: Domestic espionage and data exfiltration from construction and financial sectors in Vietnam.

objects.name: Exploit Public-Facing Application
objects.description: Suspected to have been used for initial access against the construction firm's public-facing Microsoft SQL server via an RCE vulnerability.

objects.name: Compromise Software Supply Chain
objects.description: The update mechanism of the FireAnt MetaKit stock investment application was compromised to deliver a malicious update to a select group of users.

objects.name: DLL Side-Loading
objects.description: The SPECTRALVIPER backdoor was loaded by a legitimate, signed executable to make its execution difficult for security tools to detect.

objects.name: Web Protocols
objects.description: The SPECTRALVIPER backdoor communicated with its C2 server using a domain name chosen to blend in with legitimate financial data traffic. The compromised software update also used unencrypted HTTP.

objects.name: SPECTRALVIPER
objects.description: A custom backdoor used by OceanLotus in domestic espionage campaigns. It was delivered via a supply-chain attack on FireAnt MetaKit and used for data exfiltration. It communicates with C2 over a domain designed to blend in with financial traffic.

objects.name: Construction Firm Espionage Campaign
objects.description: A year-long espionage campaign by OceanLotus against a major Vietnamese construction corporation, maintaining persistent access and likely gaining entry via an exploited SQL server.

objects.name: FireAnt MetaKit Supply-Chain Attack
objects.description: A sophisticated supply-chain attack where OceanLotus compromised the update mechanism of the FireAnt MetaKit stock investment application to selectively deploy the SPECTRALVIPER backdoor to stock market investors.

objects.name: Microsoft SQL Server RCE Vulnerability
objects.description: A remote code execution vulnerability in a public-facing Microsoft SQL server was the suspected initial access vector for the intrusion into the Vietnamese construction firm.

objects.name: SPECTRALVIPER C2 Server
objects.description: Command and control infrastructure used by the SPECTRALVIPER backdoor, using the domain financemachinelearning.com.

objects.name: FireAnt MetaKit Update Server
objects.description: The legitimate update server for the FireAnt MetaKit software, which was compromised by OceanLotus to deliver malicious updates.

objects.name: SPECTRALVIPER C2 Domain
objects.description: Detects network connections to the command-and-control domain used by the SPECTRALVIPER backdoor.
objects.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: SPECTRALVIPER C2 DNS Query
id: 7c8a0b3d-1e2f-4a5b-8c9d-0e1f2a3b4c5d
status: experimental
description: Detects DNS queries to the known SPECTRALVIPER C2 domain financemachinelearning.com.
references:
  - https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/oceanlotus-hits-vietnam-investors-with.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-11
tags:
  - attack.t1071.004
  - attack.command_and_control
logsource:
  category: dns_query
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    QueryName: 'financemachinelearning.com'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - None known
level: high

objects.name: Unsigned DLL Loaded by FireAnt.exe
objects.description: Detects potential DLL side-loading activity associated with the FireAnt MetaKit compromise, based on monitoring for the legitimate application loading unsigned DLLs.
objects.pattern:
title: Unsigned DLL Loaded by FireAnt.exe
id: 8d9b1c4e-2f3a-4b6c-9d0e-1f2a3b4c5d6e
status: experimental
description: Detects when FireAnt.exe loads an unsigned DLL, a potential indicator of DLL side-loading as seen in the OceanLotus campaign.
references:
  - https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/oceanlotus-hits-vietnam-investors-with.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-11
tags:
  - attack.t1574.002
  - attack.persistence
  - attack.defense_evasion
logsource:
  category: image_load
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\FireAnt.exe'
    Signed: false
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate but unsigned DLLs used by the application.
level: medium

objects.name: MS SQL Server Spawning Command Shell
objects.description: Detects behavior consistent with the exploitation of a public-facing SQL server, where the database process spawns a command shell.
objects.pattern:
title: MS SQL Server Spawning Command Shell
id: 4982eec2-d4e5-482e-9df2-b870ec2aee39
status: experimental
description: Detects the Microsoft SQL Server process (sqlservr.exe) spawning a command shell (cmd.exe or powershell.exe), which may indicate exploitation for remote code execution.
references:
  - https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/oceanlotus-hits-vietnam-investors-with.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-11
tags:
  - attack.t1190
  - attack.initial_access
  - attack.execution
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    ParentImage|endswith: '\sqlservr.exe'
    Image|endswith:
      - '\cmd.exe'
      - '\powershell.exe'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate administrative scripts.
level: high

objects.name: Vietnam's OceanLotus APT Pivots to Domestic Spying, Hits Construction and Finance Sectors
objects.description:
## Executive Summary
**[OceanLotus](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0050/)** (also known as APT32 or SeaLotus), a sophisticated threat actor aligned with Vietnamese state interests, has pivoted its operations to focus on domestic targets. A new report from cybersecurity firm **[ESET](https://www.eset.com/us/)** details two major espionage campaigns conducted between mid-2024 and March 2026. The first was a prolonged intrusion against a major Vietnamese construction corporation. The second was a stealthy supply-chain attack that compromised the update mechanism of FireAnt MetaKit, a popular stock investment application, to selectively deploy malware. In both operations, OceanLotus used its custom **SPECTRALVIPER** backdoor, signaling a strategic shift from foreign targets to domestic entities that may align with Vietnam's national priorities.

---

## Threat Overview
ESET uncovered two distinct, long-running campaigns attributed to OceanLotus:
1.  **Construction Firm Espionage (Mid-2024 – Feb 2026):** The APT group maintained persistent access to the network of a major Vietnamese infrastructure and transport construction company for over a year. The initial access vector is suspected to be the exploitation of an RCE vulnerability in a public-facing Microsoft SQL server.
2.  **FireAnt MetaKit Supply-Chain Attack (Oct 2025 – Mar 2026):** OceanLotus compromised the update server for the FireAnt MetaKit software. By replacing a legitimate update with a malicious one, they were able to deliver their backdoor to a select group of stock market investors. The attack was successful because the software's update process used unencrypted HTTP and lacked digital signature verification, allowing the attackers to perform a man-in-the-middle style attack on the update process.

## Technical Analysis
OceanLotus employed a range of sophisticated TTPs across these campaigns:
- **Initial Access:** The group likely used [`T1190 - Exploit Public-Facing Application`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1190/) against the construction firm's SQL server. For the FireAnt campaign, they used [`T1195.002 - Compromise Software Supply Chain`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1195/002/) by tampering with the update mechanism.
- **Execution & Persistence:** The primary payload, SPECTRALVIPER, was loaded using [`T1574.002 - DLL Side-Loading`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1574/002/). The backdoor was executed by a legitimate, signed executable, making it difficult for basic security tools to detect.
- **Command and Control (C2):** The SPECTRALVIPER backdoor communicated with a C2 server using a domain, `financemachinelearning[.]com`, specifically chosen to blend in with legitimate financial data traffic and evade detection by network security monitoring.
- **Targeting:** The supply-chain attack was highly targeted. Instead of deploying the backdoor to all FireAnt users, OceanLotus selectively infected only a small subset, indicating a focus on high-value individuals within the Vietnamese financial sector.

## Impact Assessment
These campaigns represent a significant strategic shift for OceanLotus, moving from foreign corporate and government targets to domestic ones. This could indicate the group is being tasked with supporting national policy, such as Vietnam's anti-corruption drive, by gathering intelligence on domestic companies and influential individuals. The supply-chain attack, in particular, demonstrates a high level of sophistication and patience, posing a severe risk to any organization or individual using the compromised software. The potential for data exfiltration could lead to insider trading, economic espionage, or blackmail.

## IOCs — Directly from Articles
| Type | Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Domain | `financemachinelearning[.]com` | Command-and-control server used in the FireAnt supply-chain attack. |

## Detection & Response
Security teams should focus on detecting the TTPs used by OceanLotus:
1.  **Network Monitoring:** Monitor for and block any outbound connections to the known C2 domain `financemachinelearning[.]com`. Use D3FEND's [`Network Traffic Analysis`](https://d3fend.mitre.org/technique/d3f:NetworkTrafficAnalysis) to hunt for suspicious C2-like traffic patterns.
2.  **Endpoint Detection:** Deploy EDR solutions to detect DLL side-loading. Monitor for legitimate applications loading unsigned or anomalously named DLLs from non-standard directories.
3.  **Software Integrity:** Use file integrity monitoring or application control solutions to verify the integrity of application updates, especially for third-party software. Look for changes in file hashes of core application components.
4.  **Log Analysis:** For the suspected initial vector, monitor MS SQL server logs for signs of exploitation or unusual queries. For D3FEND, this aligns with [`Database-level Policy Enforcement`](https://d3fend.mitre.org/technique/d3f:Database-levelPolicyEnforcement).

## Mitigation
Organizations can take several steps to defend against these types of attacks:
- **[`M1051 - Update Software`](https://attack.mitre.org/mitigations/M1051/):** Ensure all public-facing applications, such as Microsoft SQL servers, are promptly patched to prevent initial access via known vulnerabilities.
- **[`M1021 - Restrict Web-Based Content`](https://attack.mitre.org/mitigations/M1021/):** Use outbound traffic filtering to block connections to known malicious domains and untrusted IP addresses.
- **Application Control:** Implement application control policies, such as those using AppLocker or Windows Defender Application Control, to prevent the execution of unauthorized or unsigned DLLs. This corresponds to D3FEND's [`Executable Allowlisting`](https://d3fend.mitre.org/technique/d3f:ExecutableAllowlisting).
- **Vendor Risk Management:** For supply-chain threats, organizations should assess the security practices of their software vendors, prioritizing those who use secure update mechanisms (e.g., HTTPS, code signing).
